architecture.summary: The paper studies structured generation under constrained decoding, where LLMs produce JSON outputs that satisfy a predefined schema. The core mechanism treats schema-key wording as an implicit instruction channel: keys like 'step_by_step_reasoning' enter the autoregressive context and condition downstream value generation, separate from and interacting with prompt-level instructions. The system uses a constrained decoding backend (XGrammar) that masks invalid next tokens according to grammar, but the paper argues that schema keys—generated as part of the autoregressive sequence—act as instruction-bearing tokens, not merely neutral field names. Information flow is bidirectional: prompt text and schema keys both influence the model's distribution over continuations, and their effects can be complementary, redundant, or interfering depending on the model.

biblio.title: Schema Key Wording as an Instruction Channel in Structured Generation under Constrained Decoding

claims.statement: Schema-key wording is not a neutral formatting detail under constrained decoding; changing only the decoder-enforced schema key can noticeably change task accuracy.
Key-only instruction improves Qwen2.5-7B from 79.61 to 86.50 on GSM8K.
Key-only instruction decreases Llama-3.2-3B from 53.15 to 37.38 on GSM8K.
Qwen models tend to benefit more from schema-level instructions, whereas LLaMA models rely more on prompt-level guidance.
Prompt-level and schema-level instructions interact non-additively: the two channels can be complementary, redundant, or interfering rather than simply reinforcing each other.

A CoT-style schema key helps only when its semantic gain exceeds the distortion induced by grammar-constrained projection; this condition is formalized as a sufficient bound derived from Pinsker's inequality on KL divergence between unconstrained and constrained distributions.
The study focuses only on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K and Math500) and does not cover information extraction, tool use, code generation, data-to-text generation, or agent workflows.

Model coverage is limited to open-source Qwen and LLaMA families plus one Qwen-based distilled reasoning variant; observed instruction-channel sensitivities may not transfer to other model families, larger systems, or proprietary models.
